name: Kazakhstan

colors.symbolism: the eternal blue sky (Tengri); peace, tranquillity, and the unity of the peoples of Kazakhstan
the wealth of the steppe, the dawn of a new era, and a peaceful and prosperous future

description: A sky-blue field bearing in the centre a yellow sun of thirty-two rays above a golden steppe eagle with wings outspread. Along the hoist runs a vertical band of yellow national ornament.
history: Adopted on 4 June 1992, replacing the flag of the Kazakh SSR after independence. Designed by Shaken Niyazbekov; chosen from over a thousand entries in a national competition. The blue field consciously evokes the colour of the banners of the medieval Turkic khanates and the sky-god Tengri; the eagle and sun are pre-Soviet motifs of nomadic Kazakh heraldry.

features.symbolism: the endless blue sky — Tengri, the eternal sky-god of the Turkic peoples — and well-being, peace, and the cultural and ethnic unity of all peoples living in Kazakhstan

features.role: sun with thirty-two rays above an eagle
features.symbolism: the source of life and energy; wealth and abundance; the rays are shaped like grain to recall the steppes and Kazakhstan's status as a major grain-producing nation

features.role: steppe eagle (golden eagle) with outstretched wings
features.symbolism: the steppe eagle (berkut), a totem of nomadic Kazakh culture; freedom, sovereignty, and the lofty aspirations of the Kazakh people; the open wings express generosity and protection
a vertical band of traditional Kazakh ornament (koshkar-muiz, 'ram's horns') along the hoist, representing the art and culture of the Kazakh people
